refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication quality within the customer-defined packet loss acceptance criterion.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting teleoperation as a capability, and (2) meeting a specific quality metric (packet loss limit). The refined FR isolates the quality obligation in the problem domain without prescribing how (e.g., protocol selection, redundancy, buffering). The phrase 'customer-defined' remains because the acceptance criterion is a stakeholder-negotiated parameter, not a design choice. This FR is now atomic and solution-neutral.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct teleoperation trials under agreed network conditions (e.g., 4G/5G coverage, latency profile, interference scenarios). Measure end-to-end packet loss across representative mission durations and worksite locations. Confirm measured packet loss ≤ customer-defined target (e.g., ≤1%). Document network conditions and acceptance sign-off by Site Manager and Safety Manager.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The NGHE solution shall enable remote operation of heavy equipment from a tele-operator station.

FR-NGHE-009.2: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation command and feedback transmission with packet loss not exceeding the customer-defined acceptance criterion under agreed network conditions.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement adaptive packet prioritization and selective retransmission logic that monitors real-time packet loss and dynamically adjusts transmission strategy (e.g., redundancy level, frame rate, compression) to stay within the acceptance criterion.

DP2: Establish a dual-path communication architecture with independent logical channels (primary and secondary) that allows fallback routing and load balancing to reduce congestion-induced packet loss.
DP3: Deploy a forward error correction (FEC) encoding scheme at the logical level that adds redundancy to critical control frames, enabling recovery of lost packets without requiring retransmission.

DP4: Implement a network quality monitoring and reporting subsystem that continuously measures packet loss, latency, and jitter, and triggers operator alerts or graceful degradation when thresholds are exceeded.
DP5: Establish a hybrid approach combining selective retransmission for critical control messages with FEC for non-critical telemetry, tuned to the agreed network conditions and acceptance criterion.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP5: Establish a hybrid approach combining selective retransmission for critical control messages with FEC for non-critical telemetry, tuned to the agreed network conditions and acceptance criterion.

potential_sources: DP5 couples the retransmission logic to the classification of 'critical' vs. 'non-critical' messages—this classification must be defined consistently across all modules that generate or consume teleoperation frames.
DP5 depends on real-time network quality feedback (from DP4 or equivalent monitoring); if monitoring is absent or delayed, tuning parameters cannot adapt, reducing effectiveness.
FEC encoding and retransmission logic must coordinate to avoid redundant recovery attempts; this requires clear ownership boundaries and interface contracts between the two mechanisms.
coupling_check.notes: DP5 is preferred because it balances robustness (FEC for non-critical data) with responsiveness (retransmission for critical control), and it remains solution-neutral regarding specific FEC algorithms, retransmission protocols, or network paths. Coupling is manageable if message classification and monitoring interfaces are explicitly defined at the DP->IM boundary.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: The agreed network conditions are stable and predictable enough that tuning parameters (FEC redundancy level, retransmission timeout, message classification thresholds) can be set offline and validated in pre-deployment testing.

RISK: If network conditions are highly variable or adversarial (e.g., intermittent jamming, congestion spikes), static tuning may fail to maintain ≤1% packet loss; mitigation requires online adaptive tuning (increases complexity).
ASSUMPTION: 'Critical' vs. 'non-critical' message classification is well-defined by the worksite safety plan and operational policies; if ambiguous, misclassification could compromise safety.

RISK: Retransmission introduces latency; if the teleoperation loop has strict latency requirements (e.g., <100 ms round-trip), retransmission overhead may violate timing constraints; mitigation requires latency budget analysis.

ASSUMPTION: The customer-defined acceptance criterion (≤1% packet loss) is achievable given the physical network infrastructure; if the baseline loss is >1%, no DP can meet the requirement without infrastructure upgrade.

RISK: FEC and retransmission both increase bandwidth consumption; if the available bandwidth is limited, these mechanisms may saturate the link and paradoxically increase loss; mitigation requires bandwidth budgeting and compression tuning.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication quality by ensuring packet loss remains within the customer-defined acceptance criterion (≤1% under agreed network conditions) while preserving critical control message latency within [specify latency budget, e.g., <150 ms round-trip]. The solution shall employ a hybrid strategy: selective retransmission for safety-critical control frames (as defined in the worksite safety plan) and forward error correction for non-critical telemetry, with tuning parameters validated against the agreed network profile in pre-deployment testing. The solution shall include continuous network quality monitoring and operator alerting to detect degradation beyond the acceptance criterion.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Manages real-time communication protocols, message prioritization, and adaptive transmission strategies between operator and vehicle, including selective retransmission and FEC tuning based on network conditions.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Receives network condition metrics (latency, packet loss, bandwidth); transmits encoded/retransmitted messages with FEC and selective retry logic.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands and control messages; Teleoperation module classifies them as critical (selective retransmission) or non-critical (FEC).

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Outputs vehicle telemetry data; non-critical telemetry is encoded with FEC before transmission via Network Link.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Critical safety alerts are routed through selective retransmission path to ensure delivery; Teleoperation enforces priority.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: Teleoperation owns operator-vehicle communication, message prioritization, and adaptive transmission. Hybrid retransmission/FEC logic is core to real-time teleoperation. Minor diffusion: Network Link must provide condition metrics; Vehicle must accept retransmitted/FEC-decoded messages.

Moderate fit: Safety Services could enforce critical message classification, but does not own the transmission mechanism itself. Would require tight coupling to Network Link and Teleoperation for actual retransmission/FEC implementation. Diffusion: transmission logic leaks into Teleoperation anyway.

Poor fit: Vehicle is the receiver/consumer of teleoperated commands and source of telemetry, not the owner of transmission strategy. Placing hybrid protocol logic in Vehicle violates separation of concerns and creates tight coupling to network conditions. Diffusion: logic must diffuse to Teleoperation and Network Link.

Weak fit: NGHE Solution is a top-level system container. Placing DP5 here would be too broad and would not establish clear ownership. Diffusion: implementation details would scatter across Teleoperation, Safety Services, and Network Link.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the natural owner of DP5 because it is responsible for managing the operator-vehicle communication channel. The hybrid approach (selective retransmission for critical control/safety messages + FEC for non-critical telemetry) is a communication protocol strategy that directly supports real-time teleoperation quality-of-service. Teleoperation receives Tasking (operator commands) and Telemetry (vehicle state), classifies messages by criticality, applies the appropriate transmission strategy, and exchanges encoded/retransmitted data via the Network Link interface. This keeps the adaptive transmission logic cohesive and avoids scattering it across multiple modules.
containment_risks: Network Link must provide real-time condition metrics (latency, packet loss, bandwidth) to Teleoperation so that FEC and retransmission parameters can be tuned dynamically. If Network Link does not expose these metrics clearly, Teleoperation cannot implement the 'tuned to agreed network conditions' aspect of DP5.

Safety Services must define which messages are 'critical' (e.g., emergency stop, safety alerts) so that Teleoperation can apply selective retransmission. If Safety Services does not provide a clear criticality classification interface, Teleoperation may misclassify messages.

Vehicle must correctly decode FEC-encoded telemetry and handle out-of-order or duplicate retransmitted control messages. If Vehicle does not implement robust decoding and deduplication, the DP5 strategy will fail at the receiver.

Acceptance criterion (e.g., maximum latency, minimum delivery probability) must be agreed and communicated to Teleoperation so it can tune retransmission and FEC parameters. If acceptance criterion is vague or changes dynamically, Teleoperation cannot optimize the hybrid strategy.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The acceptance criterion (packet loss ≤ 1% under agreed network conditions) is quantifiable and measurable at the Teleoperation module boundary via Network Link metrics (latency, packet loss, bandwidth). End-to-end verification can be performed by monitoring delivery probability of critical control messages and non-critical telemetry through the hybrid retransmission/FEC pipeline. Test harnesses can inject network faults and validate that the adaptive strategy maintains ≤ 1% packet loss.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. The FR is allocated to the Teleoperation module (IM), which owns the hybrid communication strategy (DP5). Teleoperation receives operator commands (Tasking), vehicle state (Telemetry), network conditions (Network Link), and safety criticality guidance (Safety Services), then applies selective retransmission and FEC tuning to maintain the acceptance criterion. Ownership boundary is well-defined.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP5 (hybrid selective retransmission + FEC tuned to network conditions) directly addresses the FR requirement to maintain teleoperation communication quality within the packet loss acceptance criterion. The DP is solution-complete: it specifies both the mechanism (selective retransmission for critical, FEC for non-critical) and the tuning principle (to agreed network conditions and acceptance criterion). No logical gap between requirement and design parameter.

diffusion_risks: Network Link interface must expose real-time condition metrics (latency, packet loss, bandwidth) with sufficient granularity and update frequency for Teleoperation to dynamically tune FEC parameters and retransmission thresholds. If metrics are delayed, coarse-grained, or unavailable, Teleoperation cannot implement the 'tuned to agreed network conditions' aspect of DP5. Risk: DP logic diffuses into Network Link if condition sensing is not cleanly separated.

Safety Services must provide a clear, authoritative criticality classification interface (e.g., message type → criticality level) so that Teleoperation can deterministically route messages to selective retransmission vs. FEC paths. If criticality classification logic leaks into Teleoperation or is ambiguous, the DP5 strategy becomes fragile and diffused. Risk: Teleoperation may duplicate or override safety criticality logic.

Vehicle module must implement robust FEC decoding and deduplication logic to handle out-of-order or duplicate retransmitted control messages. If Vehicle does not correctly decode FEC-encoded telemetry or deduplicate retransmitted commands, the DP5 strategy fails at the receiver. Risk: DP5 logic is incomplete without Vehicle-side implementation; ownership boundary becomes blurred.

Acceptance criterion (packet loss ≤ 1%) must be hardcoded or dynamically communicated to Teleoperation at initialization/configuration time. If the criterion is vague, customer-defined but not formalized, or changes at runtime without Teleoperation awareness, the module cannot tune parameters correctly. Risk: DP5 tuning becomes ad-hoc and non-verifiable.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves 0.92 containment score with 0.08 diffusion. The DP5 logic is well-contained: message prioritization, selective retransmission scheduling, FEC encoding/parameter tuning, and adaptive transmission strategy are all core responsibilities of real-time teleoperation. Minor diffusion is acceptable and expected: Network Link must provide condition metrics (external dependency), Safety Services must classify criticality (external policy), and Vehicle must decode/deduplicate (receiver responsibility). These are interface contracts, not logic diffusion. The Teleoperation module should NOT own network condition sensing, safety policy definition, or vehicle-side decoding; it should own the decision logic and transmission orchestration. Current allocation is sound.

recommended_revisions: Formalize the Network Link interface contract: specify the schema, update frequency, and accuracy of network condition metrics (latency, packet loss, bandwidth) that Teleoperation requires. Define SLAs for metric availability and staleness tolerance.

Establish a Safety Services → Teleoperation interface for message criticality classification. Define a criticality enum or lookup table (e.g., CRITICAL, NON_CRITICAL) and ensure Teleoperation receives this classification for every incoming Tasking message and outgoing SafetyAlert. Document the ownership boundary: Safety Services defines policy; Teleoperation applies it.

Specify the acceptance criterion formally in the DP5 design document: packet loss ≤ 1% (or customer-defined target), maximum latency tolerance, and minimum delivery probability for critical messages. Include tuning parameters (FEC code rate, retransmission timeout, retry limit) and their mapping to network conditions.

Define the Vehicle module's FEC decoding and deduplication contract: specify supported FEC codes, expected message ordering guarantees, and duplicate detection mechanism. Ensure Vehicle-side implementation is verified independently.

Add a configuration/initialization interface to Teleoperation that allows the acceptance criterion and network condition thresholds to be set at deployment time. Document how Teleoperation adapts if network conditions degrade beyond the agreed range (e.g., graceful degradation, alert to operator).

Include a monitoring and telemetry output from Teleoperation that reports actual packet loss, delivery probability, FEC overhead, and retransmission rate. This enables runtime verification against the acceptance criterion and supports adaptive tuning feedback loops.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, FR-DP, DP-IM) pass with high confidence, and the DP is solution-complete. However, the recommended_revisions identify critical interface contracts and formalization gaps that must be resolved before decomposition can proceed safely. The diffusion risks are not logic failures but rather unspecified interface boundaries that could cause runtime failures or verification gaps. Given the high safety criticality of teleoperation, these interface contracts must be hardened at the current level before deriving sub-FRs. Proceeding to decomposition without formalizing the Network Link metrics interface, Safety Services criticality classification contract, Vehicle-side FEC/deduplication spec, and acceptance criterion formalization would create ambiguity that propagates downward and complicates verification. REVISE_CURRENT_LEVEL ensures these contracts are explicit and testable before sub-FR derivation.

revise.why: DP5 (hybrid selective retransmission + FEC tuned to network conditions) is logically sound but operationally incomplete. The revision must formalize the interface contracts and acceptance criterion that DP5 depends on: (1) Network Link interface schema and SLA for condition metrics (latency, packet loss, bandwidth update frequency); (2) Safety Services criticality classification interface (enum or lookup contract); (3) Acceptance criterion formalization (packet loss ≤ 1%, latency tolerance, delivery probability targets, tuning parameter ranges); (4) Vehicle module FEC decoding and deduplication contract (supported codes, ordering guarantees, duplicate detection); (5) Configuration/initialization interface for acceptance criterion and network thresholds; (6) Monitoring/telemetry output specification for runtime verification (actual packet loss, delivery probability, FEC overhead, retransmission rate). These revisions do not change the core DP logic but make it operationally precise and verifiable. The revised DP5 should read: 'Maintain teleoperation communication quality (packet loss ≤ [criterion]) by implementing hybrid selective retransmission (for CRITICAL messages per Safety Services classification) and adaptive FEC (for NON_CRITICAL messages), tuned dynamically to real-time network conditions (latency, packet loss, bandwidth) provided by Network Link interface, with Vehicle-side FEC decoding and deduplication per agreed contract, and continuous monitoring of actual delivery probability against acceptance criterion.'

assumptions: Network Link module will provide real-time condition metrics (latency, packet loss, bandwidth) with sufficient granularity and update frequency (assumed ≤ 100 ms staleness for adaptive tuning).
Safety Services module will provide a deterministic, authoritative criticality classification interface (CRITICAL vs. NON_CRITICAL) for all Tasking messages and SafetyAlerts.
Vehicle module will implement robust FEC decoding (supporting agreed code rates) and deduplication logic to handle out-of-order and duplicate retransmitted control messages.

Acceptance criterion (packet loss ≤ 1%) is fixed at design time or formally communicated to Teleoperation at initialization; runtime changes to criterion are not expected or are handled via explicit reconfiguration.
Operator and vehicle are willing to tolerate the latency and overhead introduced by selective retransmission and FEC encoding (e.g., FEC overhead ≤ 10% of bandwidth, retransmission latency ≤ 50 ms).

Network conditions remain within the 'agreed range' (e.g., latency 50–500 ms, packet loss 0–5%, bandwidth ≥ 1 Mbps); behavior outside this range is graceful degradation or operator alert, not guaranteed ≤ 1% packet loss.
open_risks: Network Link interface staleness or coarse granularity could prevent Teleoperation from tuning FEC and retransmission parameters in real time, causing DP5 logic to become reactive rather than adaptive. Mitigation: Formalize Network Link SLA (metric update frequency, accuracy, availability).

Safety Services criticality classification logic could be ambiguous, incomplete, or inconsistent across message types, causing Teleoperation to misroute messages or duplicate safety logic. Mitigation: Define criticality enum and establish a formal interface contract with test cases.

Vehicle-side FEC decoding or deduplication failures could silently corrupt control messages or cause duplicate execution, violating safety and functional correctness. Mitigation: Specify Vehicle contract and verify FEC decoding and deduplication independently with fault injection tests.

Acceptance criterion (packet loss ≤ 1%) may not be achievable under all agreed network conditions (e.g., if packet loss is inherently 2% on a given link). Teleoperation cannot tune parameters to meet an impossible criterion. Mitigation: Validate acceptance criterion against network condition ranges; define fallback behavior (graceful degradation, operator alert) if criterion cannot be met.

Monitoring and telemetry output from Teleoperation may not be available or may introduce overhead that affects real-time performance. Mitigation: Specify monitoring interface and ensure it does not degrade Teleoperation responsiveness.

Configuration/initialization interface for acceptance criterion and network thresholds could be missing or poorly documented, causing deployment ambiguity. Mitigation: Define configuration schema and initialization sequence; include validation logic to reject invalid criterion or thresholds.
evidence_needed: Formal specification of Network Link interface: schema (JSON or protobuf), fields (latency_ms, packet_loss_pct, bandwidth_mbps), update frequency (Hz), accuracy (±tolerance), and availability SLA (uptime %).

Safety Services criticality classification interface: enum definition (CRITICAL, NON_CRITICAL, or extended set), lookup table or function signature, and test cases covering all Tasking message types and SafetyAlerts.

Acceptance criterion formalization: packet loss target (≤ 1%), latency tolerance (e.g., ≤ 200 ms end-to-end), delivery probability target for critical messages (e.g., ≥ 99.9%), and mapping to network condition ranges (e.g., 'achievable if latency ≤ 500 ms and packet loss ≤ 5%').

Vehicle module FEC decoding and deduplication contract: supported FEC codes (e.g., Reed-Solomon, Turbo), code rate range (e.g., 0.5–0.9), message ordering guarantees (in-order or out-of-order), duplicate detection mechanism (sequence number, hash), and test cases for FEC decoding and deduplication under fault injection.
Configuration/initialization interface specification: schema for acceptance criterion and network thresholds, initialization sequence, validation logic, and error handling for invalid inputs.

Monitoring and telemetry output specification: fields (actual_packet_loss_pct, delivery_probability_critical, fec_overhead_pct, retransmission_rate_pct), update frequency, and performance impact analysis (CPU, memory, bandwidth overhead).

Validation study: network simulation or testbed results showing that DP5 (with formalized interfaces and parameters) achieves packet loss ≤ 1% under agreed network conditions (e.g., latency 50–500 ms, packet loss 0–5%, bandwidth ≥ 1 Mbps).
